title: SAP HANA Architecture & Internals
description: SAP HANA is not just a database — it is a column-oriented, memory-optimized, multi-engine data platform that redefines how enterprise applications handle data. Unlike traditional disk-based row-stores (Oracle, DB2, SQL Server), HANA keeps data in main memory and uses columnar compression, vector processing, and parallel query execution. This section covers HANA internals from a Staff+ perspective: column vs row store internals, memory management, query execution engine, indexing, partitioning, ABAP integration, and cloud vs on-premise deployment considerations.

sections.title: HANA Column Store vs Row Store

description.value:
The fundamental architectural decision in HANA is whether to store data in a column store or row store. All modern HANA tables default to column store, but row store remains relevant for specific workloads. Understanding the internals of each storage engine is critical for performance optimization at Staff+ level.

Column Store: Data is stored column by column in main memory. Each column is a contiguous array of values, enabling dictionary compression, run-length encoding, and vectorized (SIMD) processing. Column store excels at analytical queries that scan many rows but few columns (e.g., SUM(amount) WHERE region = ’EMEA’). The columnar layout means the query engine reads only the columns needed — it never touches irrelevant columns. Dictionary compression maps values to integer IDs, reducing memory footprint by 2-10x depending on cardinality.

Row Store: Data is stored row by row, as in traditional databases. Row store is optimized for point queries (SELECT * WHERE id = 42) and transactional workloads (single-row INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE). Row store does not use columnar compression — it reads entire rows even if only one column is needed. The advantage is lower latency for single-row operations and no decompression overhead.

Staff+ Decision Framework: Use column store for (1) tables with many columns but analytical access patterns, (2) tables that are scanned for reporting or aggregation, (3) tables that benefit from compression (high cardinality columns). Use row store for (1) small configuration tables (<10K rows), (2) tables with frequent single-row updates (hotspot tables), (3) tables that need SELECT ... FOR UPDATE with row-level locking. HANA allows mixing both in the same database — the query engine decides which store to access based on the query and the table’s storage type.

decisionTree.prompt: Should this ABAP table be column store or row store?
decisionTree.firstQuestion: "What is the primary access pattern?" Analytical (many rows, few columns, aggregation) = column store. Transactional (single row, frequent updates, locking) = row store. Configuration (small, infrequent changes) = either, but row store simpler.

branches.condition: Analytical - reporting, aggregation, many rows scanned, few columns projected
branches.path: Column store (default). Enables dictionary compression, vectorized execution, partition pruning. Best for tables with >1M rows where queries access 20-30% of columns.

branches.condition: Transactional - single-record CRUD, SELECT FOR UPDATE, frequent small writes
branches.path: Row store. Lower per-row overhead for single-row operations. No compression/decompression penalty. Use for configuration tables, counters, sequence tables, session data.

branches.condition: Mixed - both analytical and transactional on same table
branches.path: Column store with deltas. HANA column store has a write-optimized delta store (in-memory) that merges into the main column store periodically. Use for tables with moderate write volume and frequent reads.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: Column store is HANA's superpower. Default to column store unless you have a proven performance problem with single-row transactional access. The delta-merge mechanism handles moderate write workloads efficiently - the real performance trap is UPDATE-heavy patterns on column store tables with no delta merge tuning.

sections.title: HANA Memory Management

description.value:
HANA is a memory-first database — all data resides in main memory (RAM). This is the fundamental reason for HANA’s speed: disk I/O is eliminated as a bottleneck for data access. However, memory is expensive and finite, which makes memory management the single most important operational concern for HANA administrators and architects.

Memory Pools: HANA allocates memory from the OS into several pools: (1) Column Store Main — compressed column data, the largest pool, read-optimized, periodically rebuilt from the delta store via delta merge. (2) Column Store Delta — write-optimized storage for inserts/updates. Delta is uncompressed and structured as L1 delta (in-memory pending) and L2 delta (sorted, partially compressed). (3) Row Store — heap memory for row-store tables, each row stored as a contiguous block. (4) Session / Query Memory — per-connection memory for query execution, result sets, and temporary data. (5) Extended Storage — warm data that is lazily loaded from SSD to memory on access.

Compression Techniques: HANA uses multiple compression techniques depending on data characteristics. Dictionary compression assigns an integer ID to each distinct value in a column and stores the ID, not the value. This is highly effective for low-cardinality columns (e.g., status, region, currency). Run-length encoding (RLE) compresses consecutive identical values into a single (value, count) pair — effective for sorted columns. Indirect encoding and cluster compression handle high-cardinality columns (e.g., amounts, quantities). LZ4 compression is applied to the final compressed block for additional space savings.

Global Allocation Limit: The parameter global_allocation_limit controls the total memory HANA can allocate. Default is 90% of host RAM. When this limit is reached, HANA either rejects new queries or starts unloading warm data to extended storage. Monitoring the ratio of allocated to total memory is critical: if the ratio exceeds 85%, the system is at risk of out-of-memory errors.

-- Monitor HANA memory consumption
SELECT HOST, PORT,
  ROUND(ALLOCATION_LIMIT / 1024 / 1024 / 1024, 2) AS ALLOCATED_GB,
  ROUND(TOTAL_MEMORY_USED / 1024 / 1024 / 1024, 2) AS USED_GB,
  ROUND((TOTAL_MEMORY_USED / ALLOCATION_LIMIT) * 100, 2) AS USAGE_PCT
FROM M_HOST_INFORMATION;

-- Per-table memory consumption
SELECT SCHEMA_NAME, TABLE_NAME,
  ROUND(MEMORY_SIZE_IN_TOTAL / 1024 / 1024 / 1024, 2) AS SIZE_GB,
  RECORD_COUNT, COMPRESSION_RATIO, LAST_MERGE_TIME
FROM M_CS_TABLES
ORDER BY MEMORY_SIZE_IN_TOTAL DESC;

sections.codeBlock:
-- HANA Memory Consumption Monitor
SELECT HOST, PORT,
  ROUND(ALLOCATION_LIMIT / 1024/1024/1024, 2) AS ALLOCATED_GB,
  ROUND(TOTAL_MEMORY_USED / 1024/1024/1024, 2) AS USED_GB,
  ROUND((TOTAL_MEMORY_USED / ALLOCATION_LIMIT) * 100, 2) AS USAGE_PCT
FROM M_HOST_INFORMATION;

SELECT SCHEMA_NAME, TABLE_NAME,
  ROUND(MEMORY_SIZE_IN_TOTAL / 1024/1024/1024, 2) AS SIZE_GB,
  RECORD_COUNT, COMPRESSION_RATIO, LAST_MERGE_TIME
FROM M_CS_TABLES
ORDER BY MEMORY_SIZE_IN_TOTAL DESC;

quantifiedTradeoff.title: HANA Column Store Compression - Dictionary vs RLE vs LZ4

variables.name: Column cardinality
variables.value: Status column: 10 distinct values over 50M rows

dictionary.monthlyPenalty: Status: 50M * 10 bits = 62.5 MB. Dictionary overhead: 10 vals * 20 bytes = 200 bytes. Total: ~62.5 MB. Compression ratio: 20:1 vs raw string.

rle.writeLatency: RLE pairs = number of value changes in sorted column
rle.monthlyPenalty: If status column is clustered (e.g., 1M 'Open' followed by 500K 'Closed'), RLE stores 2 pairs instead of 1.5M values. ~16 bytes vs 30 MB. Best when column sorted by compressed value.

calculation.breakeven: Dictionary compression is beneficial for strings with <10K distinct values. RLE only wins when column is sorted by compressed value. For high-cardinality columns, use indirect encoding or LZ4.

sections.title: HANA Query Execution Engine

description.value:
HANA’s query execution engine is a multi-stage pipeline that transforms SQL into an optimized operator graph running on the column store. Understanding the optimizer’s choices is essential for writing HANA-efficient queries — especially for ABAP developers used to databases where the optimizer is less sophisticated.

Logical Optimizer (L Optimizer): The first stage transforms the parsed SQL into a relational algebra tree. It applies logical transformations: predicate pushdown (filters as close to the table scan as possible), projection pruning (eliminate columns not needed by the outer query), view unfolding (inline CDS views), and subquery decorrelation (convert correlated subqueries to joins). The L optimizer is cost-based — it estimates cardinality for each operator and chooses the cheapest logical plan.

Physical Optimizer (P Optimizer): The second stage chooses execution strategies: hash join (large unsorted data), merge join (pre-sorted data), nested-loop join (small driving table). For aggregations: hash aggregation (group-by with many groups) vs sort aggregation (input already sorted). The P optimizer considers index availability, partition pruning, and parallelization.

Operator Graph Execution: The final physical plan is an operator DAG. Each operator is a C++ compiled function operating on columnar data with SIMD vectorization. Key operators: COLUMN SCAN (decompress and scan segments), DICTIONARY DECODE (map IDs to values), AGGREGATION (hash or sort-based), JOIN (hash/merge/nested-loop).

-- HANA EXPLAIN PLAN
EXPLAIN PLAN SET STATEMENT_NAME = 'explain_sales' FOR
SELECT so.sales_org, so.customer_id,
       SUM(soi.amount) AS total_amount
FROM sales_order AS so
  INNER JOIN sales_order_item AS soi ON so.doc_id = soi.doc_id
WHERE so.created_at BETWEEN '2025-01-01' AND '2025-12-31'
  AND so.sales_org IN ('1000', '2000', '3000')
GROUP BY so.sales_org, so.customer_id
HAVING SUM(soi.amount) > 10000;

-- View the plan
SELECT OPERATOR_NAME, OPERATOR_DETAILS, EXECUTION_ENGINE,
       OUTPUT_SIZE, CPU_COST, MEMORY_SIZE
FROM EXPLAIN_PLAN_TABLE
WHERE STATEMENT_NAME = 'explain_sales'
ORDER BY OPERATOR_POS;

/*
  Staff+ Tips:
  - Predicate pushdown: filters on created_at AND sales_org both pushed
  - If sales_org is high-selectivity (<5%), HANA uses index-based access
  - If created_at range covers >20% of table, HANA chooses full column scan
  - For >10M rows, consider partition pruning on created_at (monthly)
*/

sections.codeBlock:
-- HANA EXPLAIN PLAN
EXPLAIN PLAN SET STATEMENT_NAME = 'explain_sales' FOR
SELECT so.sales_org, so.customer_id,
       SUM(soi.amount) AS total_amount
FROM sales_order AS so
  INNER JOIN sales_order_item AS soi ON so.doc_id = soi.doc_id
WHERE so.created_at BETWEEN '2025-01-01' AND '2025-12-31'
  AND so.sales_org IN ('1000', '2000', '3000')
GROUP BY so.sales_org, so.customer_id
HAVING SUM(soi.amount) > 10000;

SELECT OPERATOR_NAME, OPERATOR_DETAILS, EXECUTION_ENGINE,
       OUTPUT_SIZE, CPU_COST, MEMORY_SIZE
FROM EXPLAIN_PLAN_TABLE
WHERE STATEMENT_NAME = 'explain_sales'
ORDER BY OPERATOR_POS;

comparisonTable.title: HANA OLAP vs OLTP Query Optimization
rows: Full table scans, aggregations, GROUP BY, star joins
Point lookups, index seeks, single-row DML
Hash join (build hash on smaller, probe larger)
Covering indexes for star queries, bitmap for low-cardinality
Range/hash on time dimension, partition pruning
Row store or column delta (single-row updates)
comparisonTable.staffPlusInsight: HANA is optimized for OLAP. OLTP workloads can suffer in column store due to delta merge overhead. For high-throughput OLTP, consider row store tables or leverage the column store delta (uncompressed, write-optimized) with frequent delta merges scheduled during low-load.

sections.title: HANA Indexing Strategy

description.value:
HANA indexing is different from traditional databases because the column store is already indexed — each column is stored as a sorted, compressed array that can be binary-searched directly. This means many queries that would need a secondary index in a row-store database can be answered with a full column scan in HANA. The key insight: indexing in HANA is not always necessary — the column store itself provides efficient scan capabilities.

BTREE Index (Row Store): The classic B+ tree index for row-store tables. Each leaf node contains (key, rowid) pairs. BTREE is the default index type for row store, auto-created for primary key and unique constraints. Use for equality lookups and range scans on row-store tables.

CSK (Column Store Key): HANA automatically creates a CSK index for the primary key of column-store tables. The CSK is a sorted vector of PK columns with pointers to the column segment. This enables fast key-based access without a full scan — the column-store equivalent of a clustered index.

Secondary Indexes on Column Store: For non-PK columns frequently filtered with high selectivity (<5% of rows), a secondary index can improve performance. HANA secondary indexes on column store are inverted indexes — they map distinct values to a list of row IDs. Creating too many secondary indexes hurts write performance because each INSERT/UPDATE must update all indexes.

-- Create secondary index on column store
CREATE INDEX z_sales_order_created
  ON sales_order (created_at) INDEX TYPE BTREE;

-- Create composite index for common query
CREATE INDEX z_sales_order_org_status
  ON sales_order (sales_org, status, created_at) INDEX TYPE BTREE;

-- Create local index on partitioned table
CREATE INDEX z_sales_item_product
  ON sales_order_item (product_id) INDEX TYPE BTREE LOCAL;

-- Analyze index usage
SELECT SCHEMA_NAME, TABLE_NAME, INDEX_NAME, INDEX_TYPE,
       RECORD_COUNT, INDEX_USAGE_COUNT, NO_INDEX_USAGE_COUNT
FROM INDEXES
WHERE SCHEMA_NAME = 'SAPERP'
ORDER BY INDEX_USAGE_COUNT DESC;

/*
  Staff+ Notes:
  - Column store: focus on partition pruning, not indexes
  - Secondary indexes on column store: 3-5 max per table
  - Each secondary index adds ~0.5ms write overhead per INSERT
  - For point lookups, ensure CSK index is optimal
*/

comparisonTable.title: HANA Index Types - When to Use Each
rows: Default for row store. Auto-created on PK.
Auto-created for column store PK. Clustered index equivalent.
Inverted index maps value->rowids. 3-5 max per table.
Inverted term index. Search-enabled columns only.
comparisonTable.staffPlusInsight: The most common indexing mistake is over-indexing column-store tables. The column store vectorized scan is often faster than an index scan for selectivity >5%. Before adding an index, measure with EXPLAIN PLAN.

sections.title: HANA Partitioning & Scale-Out

description.value:
HANA supports two scaling models: scale-up (more RAM/CPU on a single node) and scale-out (multiple nodes in a cluster). Partitioning is the key to effective scale-out — it splits large tables across multiple nodes so queries execute in parallel. Without partitioning, scale-out is ineffective because all data must be exchanged between nodes.

Range Partitioning: Data split by a range of values, typically a time dimension. Range partitioning enables partition pruning — if your query filters on the partition key (e.g., WHERE created_at BETWEEN two dates), HANA skips irrelevant partitions. This is the most common strategy for OLAP workloads. Also enables partition sliding — dropping old partitions and adding new ones without downtime.

Hash Partitioning: Data split by a hash function (e.g., HASH(doc_id) % 16). Distributes data evenly regardless of value distribution. Ideal for OLTP workloads where access is evenly distributed. Downsides: no partition pruning — queries without the partition key scan all partitions.

Composite Partitioning: Range + hash subpartition. For example: range by month, hash by doc_id within each month. Combines pruning (month-level) with even distribution (hash within month).

-- Range partition by month
CREATE COLUMN TABLE sales_order (
    doc_id NVARCHAR(20), created_at DATE,
    sales_org NVARCHAR(10), customer_id NVARCHAR(20),
    total_amount DECIMAL(23,2),
    PRIMARY KEY (doc_id, created_at)
) PARTITION BY RANGE (created_at) (
    PARTITION '2025-01-01' <= VALUES < '2025-04-01',
    PARTITION '2025-04-01' <= VALUES < '2025-07-01',
    PARTITION '2025-07-01' <= VALUES < '2025-10-01',
    PARTITION '2025-10-01' <= VALUES < '2026-01-01',
    PARTITION OTHERS
);

-- Hash partition
CREATE COLUMN TABLE sales_order_item (
    doc_id NVARCHAR(20), item_num INTEGER,
    product_id NVARCHAR(20), quantity INTEGER,
    PRIMARY KEY (doc_id, item_num)
) PARTITION BY HASH (doc_id) PARTITIONS 16;

-- Composite: range + hash
CREATE COLUMN TABLE sales_order_wide (
    doc_id NVARCHAR(20), created_at DATE,
    total_amount DECIMAL(23,2),
    PRIMARY KEY (doc_id, created_at)
) PARTITION BY RANGE (created_at)
  SUBPARTITION BY HASH (doc_id) SUBPARTITIONS 4 (
    PARTITION '2025-01-01' <= VALUES < '2025-07-01',
    PARTITION '2025-07-01' <= VALUES < '2026-01-01',
    PARTITION OTHERS
  );

sections.codeBlock:
-- Range partition by month
CREATE COLUMN TABLE sales_order (
    doc_id NVARCHAR(20), created_at DATE,
    sales_org NVARCHAR(10), customer_id NVARCHAR(20),
    total_amount DECIMAL(23,2),
    PRIMARY KEY (doc_id, created_at)
) PARTITION BY RANGE (created_at) (
    PARTITION '2025-01-01' <= VALUES < '2025-04-01',
    PARTITION '2025-04-01' <= VALUES < '2025-07-01',
    PARTITION '2025-07-01' <= VALUES < '2025-10-01',
    PARTITION OTHERS
);

-- Hash partition
CREATE COLUMN TABLE sales_order_item (
    doc_id NVARCHAR(20), item_num INTEGER,
    PRIMARY KEY (doc_id, item_num)
) PARTITION BY HASH (doc_id) PARTITIONS 16;

quantifiedTradeoff.title: Scale-Up vs Scale-Out - Cost vs Performance

calculation.formula: TotalCost = HardwareCost + SoftwareLicensing + Operations over 3 years

scale_up.writeLatency: 2 TB RAM x 64 cores. Single node, no network overhead.
scale_up.monthlyPenalty: $47K/mo - Simpler ops (1 node). 200 queries at 500ms avg. Single point of failure. Max 6 TB RAM.

scale_out.writeLatency: 4 x 512 GB nodes. Network overhead for cross-node joins (10-30% slower for distributed queries).
scale_out.monthlyPenalty: $58K/mo - 4 nodes to monitor. Better fault tolerance. N-1 redundancy. Scales to 24 TB.

calculation.breakeven: At <4 TB compressed data, scale-up is cheaper and simpler. At >4 TB, scale-out distributes memory pressure. OLTP prefers scale-up (cross-node joins hurt). OLAP benefits from parallel partition scans in scale-out.

sections.title: HANA & ABAP Integration

description.value:
The integration between ABAP and HANA is SAP’s most critical architectural layer. ABAP communicates with HANA via SQLDBS (Database Shared Library) — a C++ library providing a high-performance, columnar-aware SQL interface. Unlike traditional databases where ABAP used ODBC/JDBC, SQLDBS is tuned for HANA’s column store and understands CDS view metadata, AMDP procedures, and ABAP Dictionary types.

Code Pushdown: The key architectural principle is code pushdown — instead of bringing data to the application server for processing, push computation to the database. ABAP SQL pushdown means the database receives the complete query (WHERE, JOIN, GROUP BY) and returns only the minimal result set. AMDP (ABAP Managed Database Procedures) takes this further by allowing SQLScript execution directly in HANA’s engine.

Database Procedure Proxy (DBProc): For scenarios where ABAP needs HANA-native features not exposed via CDS or AMDP, DBProc provides a bridge. It allows calling HANA procedures, functions, and native SQL (via ADBC or EXEC SQL) directly from ABAP. This is the escape hatch for HANA-specific features like spatial, graph, or predictive libraries.

Session Management: Each ABAP work process maintains a dedicated database connection via SQLDBS. The ABAP LUW maps to HANA transactions: SAP LUW BEGIN -> HANA BEGIN, COMMIT WORK -> HANA COMMIT. Understanding this mapping is critical for debugging lock contention and transaction isolation.

@EndUserText.label: 'AMDP Code Pushdown Example'
class ZCL_AMDP_SALES_CALC definition
  public final create public.
  public section.
    interfaces IF_AMDP_MARKER_HDB.
    types:
      begin of ty_sales_summary,
        sales_org   type vkorg,
        customer_id type kunnr,
        total_qty   type menge_d,
        total_amt   type netwr,
        order_count type i,
      end of ty_sales_summary,
      tt_sales_summary type standard table of ty_sales_summary.
    methods GET_SALES_SUMMARY
      importing
        value(it_sales_org) type string_table
        value(iv_from_date) type d
        value(iv_to_date)   type d
      exporting
        value(et_result)    type tt_sales_summary.
endclass.

class ZCL_AMDP_SALES_CALC implementation.
  method GET_SALES_SUMMARY by database procedure
    for hdb language sqlscript options read-only.
    et_result =
      select so.vkorg, so.kunnr,
             sum(soi.fkimg) as total_qty,
             sum(soi.netwr) as total_amt,
             count(distinct so.vbeln) as order_count
      from vbak as so
        inner join vbap as soi on so.vbeln = soi.vbeln
      where so.erdat between :iv_from_date and :iv_to_date
        and so.vkorg in ( :it_sales_org )
      group by so.vkorg, so.kunnr
      having sum(soi.netwr) > 10000;
  endmethod.
endclass.

/*
  Staff+ Notes:
  - AMDP runs entirely in HANA's SQLScript engine
  - No data transferred to ABAP until result returned
  - For 1M+ rows aggregated to 200: ~500ms SQLScript vs 5s ABAP
  - Options READ-ONLY: enables parallel execution
*/

sections.codeBlock:
@EndUserText.label: 'AMDP Code Pushdown'
class ZCL_AMDP_SALES_CALC definition public final create public.
  public section.
    interfaces IF_AMDP_MARKER_HDB.
    types:
      begin of ty_sales_summary,
        sales_org type vkorg, customer_id type kunnr,
        total_qty type menge_d, total_amt type netwr,
        order_count type i,
      end of ty_sales_summary,
      tt_sales_summary type standard table of ty_sales_summary.
    methods GET_SALES_SUMMARY
      importing it_sales_org type string_table
                iv_from_date type d
                iv_to_date   type d
      exporting et_result    type tt_sales_summary.
endclass.

class ZCL_AMDP_SALES_CALC implementation.
  method GET_SALES_SUMMARY by database procedure
    for hdb language sqlscript options read-only.
    et_result = select so.vkorg, so.kunnr,
      sum(soi.fkimg) as total_qty, sum(soi.netwr) as total_amt,
      count(distinct so.vbeln) as order_count
    from vbak as so inner join vbap as soi on so.vbeln = soi.vbeln
    where so.erdat between :iv_from_date and :iv_to_date
      and so.vkorg in ( :it_sales_org )
    group by so.vkorg, so.kunnr
    having sum(soi.netwr) > 10000;
  endmethod.
endclass.

adversarialResponse.challenge: "Our ABAP code is already fast enough. Moving logic to HANA via AMDP adds complexity and makes debugging harder. Why push code down?"
adversarialResponse.badResponse: "Because HANA is faster. Just convert SELECT/LOOP patterns to AMDP." (dismissive of debugging concerns)
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: "You’re right that code pushdown adds complexity — AMDP procedures are harder to trace in ST05 and can’t be debugged in SE38. However, the performance math justifies it at scale. Your current pattern loads 100K items into ABAP, loops with business logic, and updates 50K rows individually. That’s 50K roundtrips at 2ms each = 100 seconds of network latency alone. Moving the same logic to a single AMDP executes in ~200ms — a 500x improvement. For debugging: (1) add SQLScript logging via APPLOG, (2) use HANA PlanViz for AMDP tracing, (3) keep AMDPs small and focused — each procedure does exactly one transformation. The right pattern is surgical: identify the specific loop that moves data across the network, convert that single loop to an AMDP, and keep the rest unchanged."
adversarialResponse.pattern: quantified-tradeoff-with-mitigation - acknowledge the operational pain, quantify the performance delta, then offer concrete mitigation strategies.

sections.title: HANA Cloud vs On-Premise

description.value:
The decision between HANA Cloud (managed by SAP) and HANA on-premise (self-managed) is one of the most consequential choices for SAP customers. HANA Cloud is SAP’s fully managed database-as-a-service on BTP, AWS, Azure, and GCP. On-premise gives full control over hardware and configuration — but requires your own infrastructure and DB admin team.

HANA Cloud advantages: No infrastructure management, automated backups and patching, built-in HA/DR, consumption-based pricing, automatic scaling with stop/start for dev/test, built-in ML integration (PAL/APL). The service handles all OS-level and HANA-level maintenance.

On-premise advantages: Full control over hardware (dedicated NUMA, persistent memory), no data residency concerns, predictable licensing (per-terabyte perpetual), ability to customize kernel parameters beyond Cloud limits, no network latency to S/4HANA in same DC.

Staff+ Decision Framework: Three factors determine the choice: (1) Data volume — below 4 TB compressed, Cloud is cost-competitive; above 8 TB, on-prem licensing is cheaper. (2) Operations maturity — no HANA DBAs? Cloud eliminates the need for specialized HANA admin skills. (3) Latency requirements — if S/4HANA and HANA must be co-located with sub-millisecond latency, on-prem is the only option.

-- Check HANA version and deployment type
SELECT HOST, START_TIME, VERSION, USAGE
FROM M_DATABASE;

-- HANA Cloud specific views
SELECT SERVICE_NAME, PHYSICAL_MEMORY_SIZE,
       LOGICAL_MEMORY_SIZE, ALLOCATION_LIMIT
FROM M_SERVICE_MEMORY;

-- On-premise specific: check configuration parameters
-- (Cloud restricts access to these)
SELECT * FROM M_INIFILE_CONTENTS
WHERE FILE_NAME = 'global.ini'
  AND SECTION = 'memorymanager'
  AND KEY = 'global_allocation_limit';

comparisonTable.title: HANA Cloud vs On-Premise - Decision Matrix
comparisonTable.staffPlusInsight: HANA Cloud removes operational overhead at a premium. At scale, on-premise total cost is 30-40% lower. The decision often comes down to: does your organization have HANA DBAs? If not, Cloud is the pragmatic choice despite higher per-GB cost.

organizationalDimension.title: HANA Cloud vs On-Premise - Organizational Impact

aspects.name: DBA Team
aspects.responsibility: Cloud: minimal HANA-specific DBA skills needed. On-prem: requires dedicated HANA DBAs (2-3 FTE for production)
aspects.challenge: HANA DBAs are expensive and scarce ($180K+ annual). Cloud eliminates this hiring need.

aspects.name: Finance / Procurement
aspects.responsibility: Cloud: OPEX budget, predictable monthly cost. On-prem: CAPEX for hardware + license, 3-year depreciation cycle
aspects.challenge: Cloud consumption can spike. On-prem requires large upfront investment ($500K+) but lower TCO over 5 years.

aspects.name: Compliance / Audit
aspects.responsibility: Cloud: data must stay in approved regions. On-prem: data stays in customer-controlled data center
aspects.challenge: Financial services and government often require on-premise for data sovereignty. Cloud requires BTP region mapping and contractual data handling agreements.

organizationalDimension.staffPlusInsight: The Cloud vs on-premise decision is 30% technical and 70% organizational. Most enterprises end up with hybrid: Cloud for dev/test/DR and on-premise for production. The Staff+ architect plans for both and designs data synchronization (HANA replication) between them.

sections.title: Staff+ HANA Performance Patterns

description.value:
At Staff+ level, HANA performance optimization is not about writing faster SQL — it’s about identifying systemic anti-patterns, building monitoring dashboards, and creating proactive alerting. The most expensive performance problems are the ones you don’t know about until users complain.

Common Anti-Patterns: (1) N+1 SELECT in ABAP loops — reading the database in a LOOP with SELECT SINGLE. Each iteration creates a separate DB roundtrip. Fix: use FOR ALL ENTRIES or CDS association. (2) Missing WHERE clauses — SELECT without WHERE scans the entire table. Fix: always specify filters even if it’s just a dummy condition. (3) Over-indexing — creating too many secondary indexes on column store tables hurts write performance. Fix: analyze index usage with INDEX_USAGE_COUNT and drop unused indexes. (4) Delta merge neglect — column store delta grows unbounded, causing read performance degradation. Fix: schedule MERGE DELTA during low-load periods.

Query Tuning Checklist: (1) Run EXPLAIN PLAN and check for full table scans on large tables. (2) Verify predicates are pushed to the table scan level. (3) Check join order — the driver table should be the most selective. (4) Monitor memory consumption per query — if a query exceeds 1 GB of session memory, it needs optimization. (5) Check partition pruning — the WHERE clause should filter on the partition key.

-- Performance analysis queries

-- 1. Top memory-consuming queries (last hour)
SELECT TOP 20
  START_TIME, USER_NAME, STATEMENT_STRING,
  ROUND(MEMORY_SIZE / 1024 / 1024, 2) AS MEM_MB,
  EXECUTION_TIME / 1000 AS EXEC_SEC,
  CPU_TIME / 1000 AS CPU_SEC,
  PLAN_ID
FROM M_ACTIVE_STATEMENTS
ORDER BY MEMORY_SIZE DESC;

-- 2. Index usage analysis
SELECT SCHEMA_NAME, TABLE_NAME, INDEX_NAME,
       INDEX_TYPE, INDEX_USAGE_COUNT,
       NO_INDEX_USAGE_COUNT
FROM INDEXES
WHERE NO_INDEX_USAGE_COUNT > INDEX_USAGE_COUNT
ORDER BY NO_INDEX_USAGE_COUNT DESC;

-- 3. Delta merge status
SELECT SCHEMA_NAME, TABLE_NAME,
       SUM(DELTA_RECORD_COUNT) AS TOTAL_DELTA,
       SUM(MERGED_RECORD_COUNT) AS TOTAL_MERGED,
       MAX(LAST_MERGE_TIME) AS LAST_MERGE
FROM M_CS_TABLES
WHERE DELTA_RECORD_COUNT > 0
GROUP BY SCHEMA_NAME, TABLE_NAME
HAVING SUM(DELTA_RECORD_COUNT) > 100000
ORDER BY TOTAL_DELTA DESC;

-- 4. Lock contention
SELECT HOST, PORT, CONNECTION_ID, USER_NAME,
       TRANSACTION_ID, LOCK_WAIT_TIME,
       OBJECT_TYPE, OBJECT_NAME
FROM M_LOCKS
WHERE LOCK_WAIT_TIME > 10000
ORDER BY LOCK_WAIT_TIME DESC;

sections.codeBlock:
-- Top memory-consuming queries
SELECT TOP 20
  START_TIME, USER_NAME, STATEMENT_STRING,
  ROUND(MEMORY_SIZE / 1024/1024, 2) AS MEM_MB,
  EXECUTION_TIME / 1000 AS EXEC_SEC
FROM M_ACTIVE_STATEMENTS
ORDER BY MEMORY_SIZE DESC;

-- Index usage
SELECT SCHEMA_NAME, TABLE_NAME, INDEX_NAME,
       INDEX_TYPE, INDEX_USAGE_COUNT, NO_INDEX_USAGE_COUNT
FROM INDEXES
WHERE NO_INDEX_USAGE_COUNT > INDEX_USAGE_COUNT
ORDER BY NO_INDEX_USAGE_COUNT DESC;

-- Delta merge status
SELECT SCHEMA_NAME, TABLE_NAME,
       SUM(DELTA_RECORD_COUNT) AS TOTAL_DELTA
FROM M_CS_TABLES
WHERE DELTA_RECORD_COUNT > 0
GROUP BY SCHEMA_NAME, TABLE_NAME
HAVING SUM(DELTA_RECORD_COUNT) > 100000
ORDER BY TOTAL_DELTA DESC;

-- Lock contention
SELECT USER_NAME, CONNECTION_ID, LOCK_WAIT_TIME,
       OBJECT_TYPE, OBJECT_NAME
FROM M_LOCKS
WHERE LOCK_WAIT_TIME > 10000
ORDER BY LOCK_WAIT_TIME DESC;

decisionTree.prompt: My query is slow — what should I check first?
decisionTree.firstQuestion: "Is the query CPU-bound or memory-bound?" High CPU = decompression/aggregation. High memory = joins/group-by blow up. Check via M_ACTIVE_STATEMENTS (CPU_TIME vs MEMORY_SIZE).

branches.condition: CPU-bound (CPU_TIME >> EXECUTION_TIME suggests parallelism) - column scan dominates
branches.path: Check partition pruning: is the WHERE clause filtering on partition key? If not, add partition range. Check column compression ratio in M_CS_TABLES. Consider adding secondary index on high-selectivity filter column (<5% selectivity).

branches.condition: Memory-bound (MEMORY_SIZE > 500 MB) - hash join or aggregation blowing up
branches.path: Check join order: is the smaller table the driver? Use HINT (HASH_JOIN) or rewrite as nested-loop if one table is small. For GROUP BY, ensure aggregation column has low cardinality. Add LIMIT to prevent full result set materialization.

branches.condition: I/O-bound (waiting on disk) - extended storage or delta merge
branches.path: Check delta record count in M_CS_TABLES. If delta > 10% of total, schedule delta merge. Check extended storage usage: if warm data is paged in, consider increasing global_allocation_limit or moving data to in-memory.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: Most slow queries are caused by three things: (1) no partition pruning, (2) over-indexing that slows writes and confuses the optimizer, (3) delta merge neglect. Check these before diving into query-level tuning. Build a daily monitoring job that alerts when delta records exceed 5% of total records.

details: Staff+ Insight: HANA is not just a database — it is a data platform that redefines the relationship between application logic and data storage. At FAANG interviews, frame HANA discussions around the columnar paradigm shift, code pushdown, and memory-first architecture. The key insight is that columnar storage changes the performance equation: what was expensive in row-store (full table scans, aggregation) becomes cheap, while what was cheap (single-row lookups) becomes relatively more expensive. The Staff+ architect optimizes at the data architecture level, not the query level.
